text.content:
In agriculture, the right tire isn’t just about traction—it’s about matching its tire construction to the right application. With modern farms relying on heavier machinery, faster transport, and precision field work, selecting the optimal tire design ensures efficiency, reduces soil compaction, and maximizes productivity. 

At MAXAM, we engineer agricultural tires with advanced radial and bias-ply constructions tailored to specific applications. Below are three key pillars to look at when choosing the best agricultural tire for your farming operation.

With modern farms relying on heavier machinery, faster transport, and precision field work, selecting the optimal tire design ensures efficiency, reduces soil compaction, and maximizes productivity.

text.content: I. Radial vs. Bias-Ply: A Foundation of Performance

rich_text.plain_text: I. Radial vs. Bias-Ply: A Foundation of Performance

text.content: The core difference lies in how tires handle load and stress:

rich_text.plain_text: The core difference lies in how tires handle load and stress:

text.content: Radial Tires

rich_text.plain_text: Radial Tires

text.content: : Use a single-layer casing that acts like a spring, evenly distributing weight across a uniform contact patch. This reduces ground slip, improves fuel efficiency, and minimizes soil compaction—ideal for 4WD tractors, harvesters, and high-speed equipment.

rich_text.plain_text: : Use a single-layer casing that acts like a spring, evenly distributing weight across a uniform contact patch. This reduces ground slip, improves fuel efficiency, and minimizes soil compaction—ideal for 4WD tractors, harvesters, and high-speed equipment.

text.content: Bias-Ply Tires

rich_text.plain_text: Bias-Ply Tires

text.content: : Built with multiple overlapping ply layers, these function as a rigid unit, like a balloon pushing tread into the ground. While durable for low-speed tasks, they lack the flexibility of radials, leading to higher soil disturbance.

rich_text.plain_text: : Built with multiple overlapping ply layers, these function as a rigid unit, like a balloon pushing tread into the ground. While durable for low-speed tasks, they lack the flexibility of radials, leading to higher soil disturbance.

text.content: Final Verdict

rich_text.plain_text: Final Verdict

text.content:
:

Ultimately, bias-ply tires work if soil compaction is not a priority. However, radial tires offer better durability and minimal soil compaction on farmland.

rich_text.plain_text:
:

Ultimately, bias-ply tires work if soil compaction is not a priority. However, radial tires offer better durability and minimal soil compaction on farmland.

text.content: In agriculture, the right tire construction balances load capacity, traction, and soil preservation. Below, we provide the key insights on how specific designs address the unique demands of critical farming tasks.
